name: Vitamin K prophylaxis for newborns
comparator: No prophylaxis, placebo, or oral regimen
summary: Vitamin K at birth markedly reduces vitamin K deficiency bleeding. Two 1960s randomized trials show large reductions in early clinical bleeding, and surveillance data show that intramuscular prophylaxis nearly eliminates the severe late form, which oral regimens and no prophylaxis do not.
description: VKDB (hemorrhagic disease of the newborn) is bleeding from the low vitamin K stores of newborns: early (≤24 h), classic (days 1–7), and late (weeks 2–12, often intracranial). A single dose at birth—intramuscular preferred—is standard prophylaxis. Clinical VKDB is rare, so most quantitative evidence is observational rather than randomized.
interpretation: Late VKDB has never been tested in an RCT, so those estimates are observational. The large NNT reflects how rare the outcome is, not a weak treatment. Where a study shows a reported risk ratio instead of a 2×2 table, the original per-arm counts are not published.
methodologyNotes: Early-bleeding figures come from the only two RCTs (Vietti, Sutherland), entered as reported risk ratios because per-arm counts are unpublished; control risks reproduce the published NNTs. Late-VKDB figures are observational (Sankar 2016), expressed as IM vs oral. Status set to favorable given consistent evidence and universal guidelines.

outcomes.label: Early clinical bleeding
outcomes.description: Early/classic clinical bleeding in the first week (randomized trials).

outcomes.label: Late VKDB (IM vs oral)
outcomes.description: Late vitamin K deficiency bleeding, intramuscular vs. oral prophylaxis (surveillance).

outcomes.label: Biochemical deficiency
outcomes.description: Subclinical deficiency markers (PIVKA-II positivity) in randomized trials.

studies.author: Vietti
studies.citation: Vietti TJ, Stephens JC, Bennett KR. Vitamin K1 prophylaxis in the newborn. JAMA. 1960. (Effect as pooled in Sankar 2016 / Cochrane 2000.)
studies.notes: Per-arm counts not available in accessible sources; entered as the reported RR. Control risk set to reproduce the published NNT ≈ 9. Source: Sankar 2016 (PMC4862383).

studies.author: Sutherland
studies.citation: Sutherland JM, Glueck HI, Gleser G. Hemorrhagic disease of the newborn: breast feeding as a necessary factor in the pathogenesis. Am J Dis Child. 1967;113(5):524-533. (Moderate-to-severe bleeding, as pooled in Sankar 2016.)
studies.doseRegimen: Vitamin K vs placebo at birth
studies.notes: Moderate-to-severe bleeding. Per-arm counts not available; entered as the reported RR. Control risk set to reproduce the published NNT ≈ 74. Any-bleeding RR was 0.73 (0.56-0.96). Source: Sankar 2016 (PMC4862383).

studies.author: Sankar (IM vs single oral)
studies.citation: Sankar MJ, et al. Vitamin K prophylaxis for prevention of vitamin K deficiency bleeding: a systematic review. J Perinatol. 2016;36(Suppl 1):S29-S35.
studies.doseRegimen: Single IM dose vs single oral dose
studies.notes: Inverted from the published single-oral-vs-IM RR 24.5 (7.4-81.0). Observational. Control (single oral) late-VKDB risk ≈ 1.4 per 100,000. Source: Sankar 2016 (PMC4862383).

studies.author: Sankar (IM vs repeated oral)
studies.citation: Sankar MJ, et al. Vitamin K prophylaxis for prevention of vitamin K deficiency bleeding: a systematic review. J Perinatol. 2016;36(Suppl 1):S29-S35.
studies.doseRegimen: Single IM dose vs repeated oral doses
studies.notes: Inverted from the published repeated-oral-vs-IM RR 3.64 (0.82-16.3); not statistically significant. Observational. Control (repeated oral) late-VKDB risk ≈ 0.72 per 100,000 (von Kries 2003). Source: Sankar 2016 (PMC4862383).

studies.author: Sharma / Ulusahin (Cochrane)
studies.citation: Puckett RM, Offringa M. Prophylactic vitamin K for vitamin K deficiency bleeding in neonates. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2000;(4):CD002776. (PIVKA-II, IM vs control: Sharma 1995, Ulusahin 1996.)
studies.notes: Pooled PIVKA-II positivity from two randomized trials in the Cochrane review. Per-arm counts not exposed in the accessible HTML.

studies.author: Cochrane (oral PIVKA-II)
studies.citation: Puckett RM, Offringa M. Prophylactic vitamin K for vitamin K deficiency bleeding in neonates. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2000;(4):CD002776. (PIVKA-II at 3 days, oral vs control, 3 trials, n=118.)
studies.notes: Pooled PIVKA-II positivity at 3 days, oral vs control, from the Cochrane review (3 trials, n=118).
